sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 38-year-old male, presents with a 2-3 week history of right shoulder pain, which began after a series of physical projects in his backyard.
He describes the pain as sore and tender to the touch, particularly on the outside of the shoulder, and worsens with movement.
He reports no restriction in arm movement, but pain is exacerbated by activity.
He has a history of occasional pain in the same shoulder from a previous injury while playing baseball.
He has been taking Advil and applying ice to alleviate symptoms.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of occasional shoulder pain from a previous injury, but no other relevant medical history or social history was mentioned.
Review of systems: The patient reports no numbness or tingling in his arm or hands.
Current medications: The patient is taking Advil and has been instructed to continue taking it.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 116/84, pulse rate 62, respirations 16, oxygen saturation 99%, temperature 97.8.
Physical exam: The patient has limited active and passive range of motion in the right shoulder, with tenderness at the greater tuberosity of the humerus.
No tenderness is noted at the sternoclavicular or acromioclavicular joints.
The patient has a good hand grip, and neurovascular exam of the right arm shows capillary refill less than 3 seconds and intact sensation to touch.
Test Results: The patient's shoulder X-ray shows no fracture.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient's symptoms are most likely due to rotator cuff tendinopathy, which is an injury to the tendons of the muscles that help make up the shoulder joint.
Problem list: Rotator cuff tendinopathy; confirmed.
Plan:
I prescribed Tylenol for pain management.
I ordered an MRI to further evaluate the patient's shoulder.
I referred the patient to physical therapy for approximately 6-8 weeks to address the rotator cuff tendinopathy.
I advised the patient to continue taking Tylenol and to consider a steroid injection if symptoms do not improve.
I recommended that the patient soak his feet in Epsom salts to alleviate soreness and stiffness in his feet and ankles.
Follow-up: The patient will follow up in approximately 6-8 weeks to assess progress with physical therapy and to consider further treatment options.
